description: Fix the perfect audit system so each run finds a comprehensive set of issues instead of 1-2 per run. The root cause is that perfect-prompt.md optimizes for zero false positives at the expense of recall, contains no note-taking instructions (so findings from early files are forgotten by the time the model finishes reading), and has no collect-then-filter methodology. After this change, each perfect run should find 5-15 genuine issues and converge within 2-3 runs.

userStories.title: Rebalance perfect-prompt.md for comprehensive single-pass auditing
userStories.description: Rewrite the perfect audit shared guidelines to fix three structural problems: (1) the prompt heavily penalizes false positives which makes the model extremely conservative, reporting only 1-2 ultra-safe findings per run; (2) there are no note-taking instructions, so findings from early files are lost by the time the model finishes reading all files; (3) there is no collect-then-filter methodology, so the model self-filters during reading instead of collecting all candidates first and reviewing them afterward.
acceptanceCriteria: The 'It is better to report 2 genuine issues than 2 genuine issues plus 1 false positive' sentence is replaced with language that emphasizes completeness — a comprehensive audit with a few borderline findings is better than reporting only the 2 most obvious issues
The 'If you find zero genuine issues, that is a valid outcome' paragraph is softened to note that zero findings from a large codebase should prompt a double-check for over-conservatism

A new 'Audit Method' section exists that instructs the model to: (a) use TaskCreate to record every potential finding immediately as it reads each file, (b) NOT self-filter during the reading phase, (c) after reading ALL files, review the complete findings list and apply the Validation Checklist, (d) only then discard false positives
The Validation Checklist section still exists and still requires concrete consequences — the quality bar for what constitutes an issue is unchanged
The new Audit Method section appears BEFORE the 'What Is an Issue' section so the model sees the methodology instructions before the filtering criteria
userStories.notes:
Files:
- .ralph/perfect-prompt.md (the only file to modify)

Context:
This file is prepended to every perfect-*.sh audit prompt via run-prompt.sh (line 62-67). It defines the shared guidelines that all audit scripts follow. The current prompt creates extreme conservatism through two mechanisms:

1. Line 9 says: "It is better to report 2 genuine issues than 2 genuine issues plus 1 false positive." This tells the model the cost of a FP exceeds the cost of a missed issue.
2. Lines 57-58 say: "If you find zero genuine issues, that is a valid outcome. Do not manufacture issues to fill a quota." This further validates under-reporting.

The result: the model reads 20+ files, identifies ~10 candidate issues, aggressively self-filters to 1-2, and calls it done. Each re-run non-deterministically focuses on different files and finds a different 1-2 issues. The system never converges.

Changes:

1. Replace the conservatism language (around current line 9 in the Validation Checklist section, and lines 57-58 in the Severity and Completeness section) with language that emphasizes completeness:
   - Instead of "2+0 > 2+1": "A comprehensive audit that reports 10 genuine issues alongside 1-2 borderline ones is far more valuable than an audit that only reports the 2 most obvious. The autonomous agent receiving these findings can cheaply discard a false positive — but a missed bug ships to users."
   - Instead of the zero-findings absolutism: "If after exhaustive review you find zero issues, that is valid. But zero findings from a large, complex codebase should surprise you — double-check that you haven't been filtering too aggressively."

2. Add a new top-level section '## Audit Method' between 'Mindset' and 'What Is an Issue'. This section instructs:
   - Phase 1 (Collect): As you read each file, immediately record every potential finding using TaskCreate. Include the file path, a one-line description, and the suspected consequence. Do NOT evaluate whether it passes the validation checklist yet. Capture everything that looks like it might be an issue.
   - Phase 2 (Filter): After you have finished reading ALL files in scope, review your complete task list. For each finding, apply the Validation Checklist. Mark tasks as completed (discard) or keep them. The ones that survive become your PRD stories.
   - Phase 3 (Create PRDs): Create PRDs from the surviving findings.
   - Rationale paragraph: explain that this two-phase approach prevents context loss (findings from early files forgotten after reading 20+ files) and prevents premature self-filtering (discarding borderline findings before seeing the full picture).

3. Keep the Validation Checklist section (lines 42-49) completely intact — the quality bar for what constitutes an issue should NOT change. The checklist is good. The problem is when it gets applied (during reading vs after reading), not what it says.

4. Keep the 'What Is NOT an Issue' section (lines 29-38) completely intact.

Constraints:
- Do NOT weaken the Validation Checklist criteria. The 4-point checklist is correct. The fix is about WHEN filtering happens (after all files are read, not during), not about lowering the bar.
- Do NOT remove or weaken the 'What Is NOT an Issue' section. Style preferences, equivalent alternatives, and naming preferences are still not issues.
- Do NOT modify any individual perfect-*.sh scripts. Only modify perfect-prompt.md.
- Do NOT change the PRD Creation Rules section or the Cross-Script Scope Boundaries section.
- Do NOT change the Fix Quality section.
- Preserve the overall tone: rigorous, honest, no-nonsense. The prompt should still feel like it demands excellence — it just shouldn't demand under-reporting.
